I would appreciate clarification on what happens in this scenario, and whether the scope item continues to operate as before or if any changes occur.

entry.title: Re: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content: Tks for the very interesting comments.  A practical point from a procurement and supply chain perspective is that these concepts only work well if the underlying SAP data is stable. In many cases, the real issue is not the architecture itself, but inconsistent master data or incomplete transaction history. This leads to results that look correct technically but are not reliable in daily operations. A common pitfall is assuming that governance or guardrails can fix these issues. In reality, they can only control decisions within the limits of the available data. It is often more effective to first ensure consistent vendor, material, and demand data, and clearly define how decisions are made in the process. Once this foundation is stable, more advanced approaches can be introduced with much lower risk

entry.title: Re: Skipped Set Up Organizational Structure?how can I fix...
entry.content:
Good day @yeziyimm8 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: Since CBC is not configured yet, can I directly delete Company Code A in the customizing environment if it has no related configurations?

Answer: The safe answer is no. You should not expect to directly delete Code A from the tenant just because CBC has not yet been completed. Organisation entities like company codes are not designed for you to be able to delete them in this setup and the usual fallback is for you to stop using the entry and mark it clearly. Maybe with DO NOT USE. See 3580500 - Deletion of organizational units in the SSCUI 100066 - SAP for Me for more details.

Question 2: After deactivating it in the customizing environment, can I start over and configure Company Code A in the CBC environment, then activate and use it in the customizing environment? Or do I have to use a new Company Code B?

Answer: Also, you deactivating Company Code A does not reliably reset the ID for you to be able to reuse it in CBC. Note that reusing the same ID can still fail because the organisational unit identity already exists, and you manually customising changes can become inconsistent with CBC managed organisational structure.

3437598 - Company deletion is not possible in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me

3591436 - Unable to assign the purchase organization to company code XXXX - SAP for Me

What I suggest you should do/consider is:

  • Rename or decommission Company Code A operationally if needed, instead of trying to remove it technically.
  • In CBC, create a new company code ID and continue your setup there.
  • Going forward, use CBC as your sources for setting up your organisational structure instead of manually creating core organisation units first in the customising environment.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Creating Production Order Component Line Items via API
entry.content: You can find the documentation on the SAP API Hub (https://api.sap.com/bointerface/I_PRODUCTIONORDERTP). If you search for 'component' in the object I_PRODUCTIONORDERTP you'll find the documentation about the create operation including example code.

entry.title: Digital Procurement Transformation: A Modern Guide for Buying Organisations
entry.content:
Most organisations still recognise the familiar rhythm of traditional procurement: emails, spreadsheets, slow approvals, and a daily effort to keep things moving. It works—until it doesn’t. When a supplier fails, when spend balloons, or when a last‑minute request arrives, the gaps become visible.

Now imagine an alternative. A workplace where teams request what they need through a clear, guided process. Approvals move quietly in the background. Supplier information stays accurate without manual chasing. Leaders see spend in real time, not weeks later. This is the world that digital procurement transformation aims to create.

The transformation is not only technical. It is behavioural. It changes how organisations coordinate, decide, and respond. And when done well, it turns procurement into a steady, strategic partner.

What Digital Procurement Transformation Means in Practice

Digital procurement transformation replaces disconnected, manual activity with connected systems and evidence‑based decisions. It shifts procurement from a collection of tasks to a coherent operational system.

Three changes define the shift:

  1. Digital workflows instead of manual routines.
    Tasks that once required phone calls and spreadsheets move into structured, traceable processes.

  2. Real‑time visibility instead of retrospective reporting.
    Data becomes immediate and actionable, allowing teams to anticipate rather than react.

  3. A connected procurement environment.
    Procurement, finance, IT, and suppliers operate within the same digital landscape rather than separate channels.

This is where platforms such as SAP S/4HANA and SAP Ariba often play a role. Many organisations use them to bring sourcing, contracting, purchasing, and invoicing into a single ecosystem. The value does not come from the tool alone but from the coherence it helps create. When all procurement activity flows through one environment, people work with greater confidence and fewer surprises.

 Digital procurement is therefore not a destination. It is a shift in quality: clarity, speed, and trust.

Why Organisations Are Making the Shift Now

Three forces make digital procurement transformation more important than ever.

1. Manual processes no longer scale

Lengthy approvals, repeat data entry, and unclear spend categories all slow the organisation down. Errors accumulate. Opportunities are missed. In contrast, digital processes reduce friction and allow procurement teams to focus on value rather than administration.

2. Supply chains are more fragile

Disruptions—from storms to shortages—arrive without warning. Organisations relying on manual visibility struggle to respond. Digital systems help procurement spot early signals and assess alternative suppliers before risks escalate.

3. Expectations are rising

Most employees now expect consumer‑grade simplicity in workplace systems. Leaders want visibility they can trust. Finance wants control without slowing the business. Digital procurement is often the only way to meet all of these expectations simultaneously.

The result is a shift in identity. Procurement becomes a partner in planning, risk, and strategy—not just in purchasing.

How Organisations Transform Successfully

Digital procurement transformation works best when the organisation builds momentum through clear intent and consistent behaviour.

Set a Clear, Shared Vision

Teams need to know what the transformation is there to achieve. Is the goal faster buying? Better compliance? Improved planning? A reduction in maverick spend? Clear aims align decisions and make trade‑offs easier.

Secure Visible Leadership Support

Support from the CPO, CFO, and CIO provides direction and stability. Leaders help remove barriers and reinforce the message that new ways of working are here to stay.

Choose Technology That Fits the Organisation

The ideal digital platform supports the way the organisation works. It should integrate with finance, guide users through simple choices, and scale as needs evolve.

This is where systems like SAP Ariba often help. For example:

  • Organisations use SAP Ariba Guided Buying to give employees a simple, intuitive entry point—reducing training needs and cutting errors.
  • Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance helps centralise supplier onboarding and oversight, reducing the scattered email chains that many organisations struggle with.
  • Through SAP Ariba Contracts, teams create and store contracts in a single place, improving visibility and reducing version confusion.

These are not features for the sake of features. They address common organisational problems: fragmented information, inconsistent processes, and slow decisions.

Support People Through the Change

Technology changes quickly. Habits do not.
Training, communication, and early involvement of staff make the transition smoother. When users understand what improves for them—fewer errors, fewer steps, clearer rules—they are more willing to adopt new systems.

There is often a moment of hesitation here. A new workflow might feel slower the first time. A button is in a different place. The familiar shortcut no longer works. This moment is normal. It marks the start of new habits forming, not a flaw in the transformation.

Monitor Progress and Adjust

Digital procurement is not a one‑time installation. Organisations refine processes over time. Reviewing cycle times, automation levels, and compliance rates helps identify where improvements will have the greatest effect.

Small adjustments, made steadily, create lasting change.

Common Challenges and How to Navigate Them

Even well‑run programmes face predictable hurdles.

Unclear Goals

Without specific objectives, teams struggle to prioritise. Clear targets help everyone understand what matters most.

Resistance to Change

People worry about losing control or becoming less relevant. Involving them early and showing how the new approach reduces daily frustration builds trust.

Poor Data Quality

Digital tools rely on accurate information. Cleaning supplier records, contract metadata, and spend categories before implementation prevents downstream issues.

SAP Ariba customers often highlight how centralised data standards help: once suppliers update their details in the Ariba Network, those changes flow through automatically rather than relying on manual correction.

Integration Complexity

Connecting procurement with finance, operations, and planning can be challenging. Collaboration with IT and careful testing reduce the risk of breaks between systems.

Addressing these challenges early keeps momentum intact and avoids the spiral of workarounds that can undermine new systems.

What a Successful Transformation Looks Like

A strong digital procurement transformation brings measurable and behavioural improvements.

Faster Buying

Routine purchases complete quickly. Users rely on guided catalogues. Approvals flow without manual intervention.

Better Cost Control

Spend becomes visible and predictable. Procurement teams spot patterns and work with suppliers to achieve better value.

Greater Transparency

Data becomes reliable. Leaders see where money goes. Compliance improves naturally because systems guide people towards correct choices.

More Collaborative Supplier Relationships

Digital platforms make it easier to share information and address issues early.
Many organisations use SAP Ariba Supplier Management to standardise onboarding steps, track performance, and maintain a shared audit trail.

Scalability and Adaptability

Cloud‑based systems evolve with the business. Teams can add new categories, suppliers, or processes without major disruption.

These improvements take hold gradually. The first few weeks may feel unfamiliar. By the third or fourth month, teams begin to recognise the rhythm of the new process. Over time, the earlier manual world feels distant.

A Practical Example: A manufacturer in Construction & Engineering

A large manufacturer in Construction & Engineering faced slow cycle times, limited visibility, and inconsistent supplier management. Most processes were manual. Only a small share of spend was under contract. Approvals often stalled in inboxes.

The company introduced digital procurement step by step. It used guided buying principles, automated purchase orders, and AI‑supported spend insights. Supplier onboarding moved from scattered emails to a structured digital flow. Training focused on helping teams feel confident early.

Within a year, procurement cycle times fell sharply. Most purchase orders were automated. Supplier delivery performance improved. Compliance rose. And users—initially sceptical—reported that buying had become easier.

The interesting shift was cultural. Procurement was no longer a bottleneck. It became a source of clarity and consistency.

The Role of SAP Ariba in the Wider Journey

Digital procurement transformation can succeed with various tools, but SAP Ariba often appears in organisations seeking scalability and integration. Its value lies not in its features alone but in how it supports fundamental behaviours:

  • Clarity through intuitive guided buying.
  • Control through centralised contracts and supplier data.
  • Connection through shared processes that link procurement, finance, and suppliers.

These capabilities help organisations build consistent habits. When people trust the system, they stop inventing workarounds. And when teams share a coherent digital environment, they make better decisions together.

If SAP Ariba were removed, the principles of digital procurement transformation would remain. But with it, many organisations find those principles easier to put into daily practice.

A Return to the Opening Image

At the start, we imagined a workplace where buying is simple, transparent, and dependable. That world can feel distant, especially when teams are navigating spreadsheets and long approval chains.

Digital procurement transformation does not make that world appear overnight. But with clear goals, steady leadership, good technology, and patient adoption, the organisation edges closer each day.

There comes a moment—often quiet—when someone places an order, tracks its status, and completes their work without needing to chase anyone. And the team realises that the future they pictured at the start is no longer imagined. It is simply how procurement works now.

Kind regards

Lars Elmquist

entry.title: Delay one operation of task in maintenance plan
entry.content:
Hi!

I created a task with multiple operations.

All operations have a weekly cycle, but one of them has a 6 week cycle.

Now I want to create multiple maintenance plans which reference this task; my problem is, if I launch them at the same time, every maintenance plan will bring up the 6 week cycle operation on the same week.

Right now I see one option: launch the plans with a week delay each, this way the 6 week cycle operation will have a week delay aswell (compared to the other maintenance plans)

But is there a way to delay the operation itself when launching the plan? This way I could launch all the plans at once, but that one operation will have a delay.

Thanks in advance, I hope my question is understandable

entry.title: Re: replacement for TDMS
entry.content: Great question that I hear all the time. Synthesized is the only real modern alternative for SAP TDMS and a strong fit for S4 HANA and other SAP and non-SAP apps. Their platform is the only vendor I've seen with SAP native capabilities supporting all data generation (copying, subsetting, timeslicing, masking, scrambling, synthetic data generation, etc.).

entry.title: Re: AI driven Composite Creation in SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Hi @zee_siddique15 ,

The reason the Date field is being treated as a measure is because the values (like 202601–202652) are in a numeric format. The system is interpreting them as numbers rather than a date dimension.

To fix this, you can try formatting the Date column as a proper date (or even as a string/categorical field) before uploading, this should make it recognize it correctly as a dimension.

entry.title: Re: AI driven Composite Creation in SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Hi @andre229090 ,

Yes, it should work with BW live data models as well.

However, since live models don’t directly provide sample data to the widget assistant, you’ll need to create a small sample dataset in CSV format that reflects your structure (dimensions, measures). This acts as a reference for the assistant to correctly understand your columns and generate the widget.

Thank you,

Praneel

entry.title: Re: How to set up MaxDB for JDBC connection via TLS/SSL
entry.content:
Hello,

The steps are:

1. create PSE, personal security environment file on MaxDB Server

2. import certificate into client security environment

3. enable encryption in the java connect url

The first step is standard for all environments. It is looking like

In the following description will be assumed, that MaxDB is installed according to SAP guidelines:

Global DataPath: /sapdb/data (<drive>:\sapdb\data on Windows)

Global Programs Path: /sapdb/programs

In your case you can see the paths with 'cat /etc/opt/sdb'

Or on Windows: regedit -> HKLM\Software\SAP\SAPDB Tech\

Always use most recent MaxDB Software version!

Steps for configuration

Prerequisites

Installation of SAP Cryptographic Library CommonCryptoLib

(s. Installing of SAP Cryptographic library in SAP help)

In SAP Java J2EE system the corresponding library, libsapcrypto.so, is in $DIR_INSTANCE/exe directory. I.e.  (/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe).

Certificates configuration on Server side

Make SAP cryptographic library available on SAP MaxDB data base host in the global programs lib directory (/sapdb/programs/lib), change ownership and permissions according to other modules in this directory.

Example:

chown sdb:sdba /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

chmod 755 /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

 

Generate MaxDB Server PSE file

Certificate can be created either with sapgenpse tool, which is part of SAP J2EE installation or using SAP Web Dispatcher (s. SAP help).

Example for sapgenpse usage:

Set environment

export  SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/sapdb/programs/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH

Create PSE file

sudo -u sdb /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse gen_pse -p SDBSSLS.pse -x "" -noreq "CN=localhost"

You can use further specifications for the PSE creation, however identifier "CN=localhost" is important, because there is hostname check.

Result: the PSE file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.pse is created

Export certificate

Login as OS user <sid>adm and do the steps:

export SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse export_own_cert -o /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -p SDBSSLS.pse

Result: the certificate file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt is created.

Add MaxDB certificate to the J2EE secure store

Global secure store

As OS user <sid>adm execute the command:

keytool -import -alias "maxdblistener" -file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -keystore /usr/sap/<SID>/SYS/exe/jvm/linuxx86_64/sapjvm_8.1.091/sapjvm_8/jre/lib/security/cacerts

The tool keytool will ask for the password. If you didn’t customize it, the initial value is ‘changeit’.

Restart MaxDB communication listener

Extend LD_LIBRARY_PATH with the path of libsapcrypto :

(normally it is already in the environment of the j2ee engine user, like /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/libsapcrypto.so)

Stop listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener -all stop

Start listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener 

Check in the global working directory that SSL server was started too

cat /sapdb/data/wrk/nisslserver_<host>.prot_7270

 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Date       Time       PID   TID Typ  MsgID Label    Message Text

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12945 NISSLSRV U64/LIX86 7.9.11   Build 001-103-270-812

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12943 NISSLSRV NISSL support started.

Set encryption option for j2ee connection url

Open config tool and select from secure store, connection pool, the URL.

Extend URL string with the option: &encryption=true

Save change and close the config tool. Start it again. If you see that the tool was able read the data from DB, this means connection worked.

Restart java application.

entry.title: Re: Few important app is not available - FB01
entry.content:
Hi Brijesh,

There are two issues in your question- 

First one- Vendor1 credit and Vendor 2 debit or vice versa.

iam nt sure what is the business case that you will offset one vendor with other, This does not seem to be appropriate. But still if you want to do, you cannot do it in SAP directly. You need to use one Intermediary GL account (or clearing account) and post two entries in system. 

Entry one: Debit Intermediary GL account; credit Vendor 1

Entry two: credit Intermediary GL account; debit Vendor 2

Second case: Debot 1 GL account and credit 3 vendors Account. This also does not seem to be appropriate business case. Vendors invoice are pisted based on the PO or FI invoice submitted by vendor. Three vendors  submitting  one invoice look weird. Please recheck the business requirement again. If still you want to post, u cannot do it in one document. You need to do it as three invoices in S4 HANA.

entry.title: In the Valuation Balance app, rent increase does not update succeeding opening/closing balances
entry.content:
In the Valuation Balance app (Cash Flow Data for Valuation) report, rent increase/decrease does not update succeeding opening/closing balances.

When increasing the rent in a lease contract, the Valuation Balance app only shows the adjusted opening/ending balances of the asset/liability but does not highlight the change. This leads to the opening and closing balances not being the same in the subsequent month after the change.

Can we have a report where remeasurements like this are included?

• Lease asset: opening balance + remeasurement – depreciation = ending balance
• Lease liability: opening balance + remeasurement (should be equal to the amount in the lease asset) – payment + interest accretion = ending balance


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: In the Valuation Balance app, rent increase does not...
entry.content:
Thank you for your question!

The App, 'Valuation Balance - Information System' is capable of displaying the contract balances determined via the valuation performed. It does not account for the values impacting the balance change separately, but rather includes them in the overall balance - with some exceptions for liability difference postings, initial costs, retroactive changes etc.

If you would like to review the overall valuation flow which would also display in detail all the changes leading to the adjustment of the opening/closing balances, I can recommend utilizing App 'Display Contract Valuation' that highlights these entries as well.

In addition, you can explore building custom reports fit to your specific business requirement by exploring the released and available CDS views for Contract and Lease Management.

entry.title: AI capabilities for sanctioned party list screening within SAP GTS Edition for HANA
entry.content:
The SPL screening accuracy of GTS has improvement potentials. Official Lists of denied parties are growing and we have more feeder systems which require screening. 

AI will have to be implemented to go through the large amounts of SPL hits where over 90 % of both Business partners and documents are false positives. 

Benefit: avoid disturbing business unnecessarily. Automatic release of business partners, orders, deliveries and payments which are blocked due to false positives to reduce business handling time (within predefined confidence level).

entry.title: Announcing the First-Ever System-Based SAP Customer Data Platform Certification
entry.content:
On behalf of the Product Learning team at SAP, I am excited to introduce a new way to validate your SAP Customer Data Platform skills: a system-based certification that measures your ability to perform real tasks and scenarios in SAP Customer Data Platform. Instead of selecting answers on a theory-only exam, you’ll complete hands-on activities in an SAP-provisioned environment—demonstrating the day-to-day capabilities that matter on live sites. Full details and the latest policies are available on the certification page:

SAP Certified - Implementation Consultant - SAP Customer Data Platform

 

🌟A New Era of Practical, System-Based Assessment

Unlike standard multiple-choice exams, this new certification provides candidates with:
  • A SAP Customer Data Platform system, and
  • A PDF containing a set of realistic tasks to complete
Candidates perform actual configuration and scenario-based exercises directly in the system. Once all required tasks are completed, they can submit their results for automated evaluation. Within minutes, candidates will know whether they have passed or failed — offering a faster, more authentic, and more meaningful way to validate capability.
This practical approach ensures that certified consultants are not only knowledgeable but also skilled in applying SAP Customer Data Platform functionality in realistic customer scenarios.

🎯Why System Based Certifications?

The reimagined SAP Certification exams offer a range of benefits. More specifically:

  • Job-ready proof: Demonstrates you can navigate SAP Customer Data Platform and execute business-critical tasks—not just recall concepts.
  • Strong SAP signal: An SAP-recognised credential and digital badge that hiring managers and project leads across the ecosystem trust.
  • Practice over memorisation: Preparation aligns to SAP Learning Journeys and hands-on exercises for better retention and impact.
  • Real-world relevance: Validates cross-functional workflows (catalogue, content, search, promotions) that move conversion and revenue KPIs.
  • Always current: Content and recertification align with SAP’s cloud release cycle, reflecting the latest features and UI. 
  • Higher integrity than Q&A-only: Performance on practical tasks reduces guesswork and emphasises authentic ability.

👤 Who Is This Certification For?

This certification is designed for:

  • Consultants

It is classified as an intermediate-level certification and requires approximately 3 hours to complete.

📚How to Prepare

To set yourself up for success, we recommend the following preparation path:

1. Complete the "Implementing SAP Customer Data Platform" Learning Journey

The Learning Journey for Consultants on learning.sap.com provides the foundational knowledge required for the hands-on portion of the certification. They combine structured courses with access to practice systems for guided exercises, building familiarity with the interfaces, workflows, and configuration steps assessed.

2. (Optional) Attend the In-Person Classroom Trainings

For learners who prefer guided instruction, tan intructional led training is available:

  • SAP Customer Data Platform Implementation

This class provides expert-led, structured instruction and practice to deepen your understanding and skills.

 

📢Spread the Word!

Help us share this exciting update with partners, colleagues, and other stakeholders.

📝We Value Your Feedback

After completing the certification, please take a moment to share your feedback. Your insights help us continuously improve the learning experience.
 
Two easy ways to share feedback!
  1. Click “Share Feedback” to submit your thoughts. 

     

     

  2. In the “Notification Center” under “Learning” you can select the congratulatory email after completing course or learning journey and click “Feedback” to share your thoughts.

entry.title: Re: How do i update 3E regulatory content using Optimize
entry.content:
Hello

regarding

My client is using Optimize and i am new to using it. Please guide or share any administration manual to update.

Normally 3E delivers lot of informations.. so just ask 3E for advice.

But "Optimize" is different. Regarding property tree: if there is no property tree update as part of "Optimize": then there is no other option to do a "SP" change (Support Package). But not  often you need a new property tree to use e.g. new rule sets or new WWI templates.

The best you can do is: ask 3E for advice(e.g. for which Optimize update you would need an update in property tree.

Wish you the best

C.B.

entry.title: Law 9/2025 for Spain: The Digital Administrative Control Document for Road Transport
entry.content:
Spain's Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility, published in December 2025, mandates that the Administrative Control Document (Documento de Control Administrativo, DCA) — a paper-based document required for every public road freight shipment — must become fully digital within 10 months of the law's entry into force. This means the Electronic Administrative Control Document (eDCA) becomes mandatory by approximately October 2026. In this post, we break down what this means, how eCMR fits in, and the two compliance methods available to companies.
 

The Administrative Control Document (DCA)

The Administrative Control Document (Documento de Control Administrativo, DCA) is regulated by Order FOM/2861/2012. It is a mandatory document required for every public road transportation of goods within Spain.

Key Characteristics

  • Scope: Applicable only in Spain, for every shipment of goods by road.
  • Copies: According to Article 8, two copies must be issued — one for the loader company and one for the carrier.
  • Format: The document has no regulated format (no official template), but must contain all mandatory information.

Mandatory Information (Articles 5 and 6)

The DCA must contain, at minimum, the following information:

  • Loader (Company code). Company Name, Tax ID Number (NIF/CIF), and Fiscal Address
  • Carrier (Transporter). Company Name and Tax ID Number (NIF/CIF)
  • Place of Origin. Starting point of the merchandise transport
  • Place of Destination. Delivery point of the merchandise
  • Nature of Merchandise. Description/type of goods being transported
  • Weight. Weight of the transported merchandise
  • Date of Transport. Date when the transport takes place
  • Vehicle License Plate(s). All license plates must be reported. For articulated vehicles, all plates are required. If the vehicle changes during transport, the carrier must update this information.
  • Observations. Notes, remarks, and any other relevant information

Other Transport Documents

Understanding the DCA requires context about other transport documents in the Spanish and European landscape:

     National Consignment Note (Carta de Porte Nacional)

An optional document regulated by Order FOM/1882/2012. It records the contract details of road transport. Applicable only in Spain.

    CMR (Carta de Porte — International)

Spain is a signatory of the CMR Convention (Convenio relativo al Contrato de Transporte Internacional de Mercancías por Carretera). The CMR document serves as probative evidence of the international road transport contract. It establishes the relationship between the loader and transporter and is used as evidence of the reception of goods.

The CMR is only required for international road transports where:
  • Goods are loaded in Country A and delivered in Country B.
  • At least one of the countries is a signatory of the CMR Convention

    eCMR — The Electronic CMR

The eCMR is the electronic version of the CMR document. Spain adhered to the eCMR protocol in 2011 (Instrumento de Adhesión de España al Protocolo Adicional al Convenio relativo al contrato de transporte internacional de mercancías por carretera).

At the European level, EU Regulation 2020/1056 on electronic freight transport information (eFTI) establishes common rules for the electronic exchange of information between operators and administrations. In this context, eCMR is a key tool for the modernization of road transport through electronic means.

The CMR and eCMR documents are accepted as a valid Administrative Control Document, as long as all the mandatory information listed above is included.
 

Law 9/2025 "Ley de Movilidad Sostenible"

The Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility , published in December 2025, introduces a critical mandate in its Transient Provision Eight:

  • Digitalization of the DCA (= eDCA)
  • Mandatory Digital DCA by October 2026
  • The Administrative Control Document (Order FOM/2861/2012) must necessarily be digital 10 months after the entry into force of Law 9/2025 (October 2026)

The Ministry of Transports provides two methods to meet this requirement, published on its official website (Documento de Control Digital)

 

 

eDCA storage in the Ministry-Managed Repository

The company submits the eDCA to the Ministry of Transport on demand, upon inspection request during transport. The document is identified by an assigned code. ("Remisión de documento de control")

  • File format: PDF/A-1a
  • Maximum file size: 4 MB per file
  • The control document must be identified by the code assigned in the transport control

This process is exemplified in this video by the Ministry of Transports: Documento de Control Digital y aplicacion para su gestion. 

 

eDCA storage in the Company-Managed Repository

The company stores the eDCA on its own servers and includes a QR code embedded in the document pointing to the original document's URL. Inspectors scan the QR to access it. ("Comunicación de dominio de plataformas de documentos de control electrónicos"). Pre-requisites:

  • The EDCA must be stored in a registered repository in PDF format.
  • The company must have previously registered its storage domain with the Ministry of Transport.
  • The domain must use an HTTPS connection.
  • The QR code must point to the original eDCA document on the registered domain.

 

Electronic Signature Requirements

According to Article 4 of Order FOM/2861/2012, the Administrative Control Document must be formalized (signed) by:

  • The effective carrier company (company owning the transport authorization).
  • The contractual loader company (company that contracts the carrier for the shipment).

The Resolution by the General Directorate of Road Transports (May 22, 2023) establishes that the EDCA can be used digitally only if specific conditions are met. These conditions align with Article 26 of the eIDAS Regulation on Advanced Electronic Signatures:

  • The signature must be uniquely linked to the relevant signatory
  • The signature must allow the identification of the signatory
  • Created using means that the signatory can keep under their sole control
  • Linked to the signed data so that any subsequent modification can be detected
❌The following are NOT valid as electronic signatures:
  • Handwritten signature images inserted in the PDF document
  • Acceptance checkboxes to click
  • Any digital signatures that do not meet the Advanced Electronic Signature criteria
 

Persistence

According to Article 9 of Order FOM/2861/2012:

  • The Administrative Control Document (or a copy of it) must be kept available for inspection for at least 1 year.
  • Any kind of storage can be used, as long as the required information remains readable.
  • The eDCA stored in the Company-Managed Repository, the storage location must be accessible from outside via a URL on a registered HTTPS domain.

 

Disclaimer

This blog only intends to explain the new regulatory aspects of Law 9/2025 (Ley de Movilidad Sostenible) regarding the Administrative Control Document.
SAP is currently analyzing this requirement . To stay up to date with the latest news about this analysis, check the publication "Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility: Administrative Control Document" in the Regulatory Change Manager. The final assessment of this feature will determine whether it will be rejected, or the scope approved for development.

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation.

The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.

entry.title: Re: How to set up MaxDB for JDBC connection via TLS/SSL
entry.content:
Hi,

the securing of java connection consists of 3 steps:

1. Create server private security environment file (PSE)

2. Import the certificate to the java client

3. enter encryption property into connection url

In the following description we assume the MaxDB global Programs is located in /sapdb/programs, Global Data is located in /sapdb/data. You can find these settings in /etc/opt/sdb

Steps for configuration

Prerequisites

Installation of SAP Cryptographic Library CommonCryptoLib

(s. Installing of SAP Cryptographic library in SAP help)

In SAP Java J2EE system the corresponding library, libsapcrypto.so, is in $DIR_INSTANCE/exe directory. I.e.  (/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe).

Certificates configuration on Server side

Make SAP cryptographic library available on SAP MaxDB data base host in the global programs lib directory (/sapdb/programs/lib), change ownership and permissions according to other modules in this directory.

Example:

chown sdb:sdba /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

chmod 755 /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

 

Generate MaxDB Server PSE file

Certificate can be created either with sapgenpse tool, which is part of SAP J2EE installation or using SAP Web Dispatcher (s. SAP help).

Example for sapgenpse usage:

Set environment

export  SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/sapdb/programs/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH

Create PSE file

sudo -u sdb /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse gen_pse -p SDBSSLS.pse -x "" -noreq "CN=localhost"

You can use further specifications for the PSE creation, however identifier "CN=localhost" is important, because there is hostname check.

Result: the PSE file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.pse is created

Export certificate

Login as OS user <sid>adm and do the steps:

export SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse export_own_cert -o /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -p SDBSSLS.pse

Result: the certificate file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt is created.

Add MaxDB certificate to the J2EE secure store

Global secure store

As OS user <sid>adm execute the command:

keytool -import -alias "maxdblistener" -file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -keystore /usr/sap/<SID>/SYS/exe/jvm/linuxx86_64/sapjvm_8.1.091/sapjvm_8/jre/lib/security/cacerts

The tool keytool will ask for the password. If you didn’t customize it, the initial value is ‘changeit’.

Local secure store

keytool -import -alias "maxdblistener" -file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -keystore /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapjvm_8/jre/lib/security/cacerts

 

Restart MaxDB communication listener

Extend LD_LIBRARY_PATH with the path of libsapcrypto :

(normally it is already in the environment of the j2ee engine user, like /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/libsapcrypto.so)

Stop listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener -all stop

Start listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener

 

Check in the global working directory that SSL server was started too

cat /sapdb/data/wrk/nisslserver_<host>.prot_7270

 

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Date       Time       PID   TID Typ  MsgID Label    Message Text

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12945 NISSLSRV U64/LIX86 7.9.11   Build 001-103-270-812

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12943 NISSLSRV NISSL support started.

 

Set encryption option for j2ee connection url

Open config tool and select from secure store, connection pool, the URL.

Extend URL string with the option: &encryption=true

Save change and close the config tool. Start it again. If you see that the tool was able read the data from DB, this means connection worked.

 

Restart java app.

entry.title: Invoice ERROR
entry.content:
Here is the English translation of the SAP error message text:

Diagnosis

If the following conditions are met, invoice line items received multiple times via EDI/SOA can be settled:

  • Goods receipt–based invoice verification is defined for the purchase order line item.

  • The goods receipt number (delivery note number) is included in the IDoc or SOA message.

  • A differential invoice is used and a previous invoice already exists (as the second invoice). In other words, according to the two-line item concept, for each goods receipt line item or purchase order line item, both the current line item (invoice line item) and the line item from the previous invoice (credit memo line item) are displayed.

System Response

If these conditions are not met, posting of the IDoc or SOA message is canceled.

Procedure

Check the IDoc or SOA message. In some cases, the IDoc or SOA message can be edited (for example, if the supplier accidentally sent the wrong purchase order line item). If it cannot be edited, contact the supplier and request that they resend the invoice with corrected data.

Let me know if you need any adjustments to the translation.

entry.title: Re: Custom Domain Service in SAP BTP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
entry.content:
Hi @ChadB 

Very Good Query, below is the answer of your query:

If you try to access the URL "vendor.company.com", it will open the default site that is correct.

To open another site in same work zone instance with custom domain you have to give the site id after the host name of custom domain:

The site id you will get from the site setting as below:

Example: if your site id is "1234" the try to access the below URL:

https://employee.company.com/site?siteId=1234#Shell-home

Please note you have to maintain both the custom domain in the "Saas Routes" configuration of custom domain service. 

I hope this will resolve your issue, Please mark this as solution if it fix your issue also update in the blog.

 

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: Re: Which Idoc or RFC/ BADI / EXIT triggered to EWM while doing partial usage decision in S4

entry.title: How to Use Multiple APIs in a Single iFlow (SAP Integration Suite)
entry.content:
In real-time integration scenarios, we commonly use multiple APIs within a single integration flow (iFlow). For example, one API response is required as input for another API, or the final response may be the combination of the both API responses.

SAP Integration Suite (CPI) provides components like content modifier, adapters that helps us for multiple APIs scenarios in one iFlow.

In this blog, we will walk through a step-by-step implementation of calling two APIs within a single iFlow, capturing each response, and finally combining or processing the data before sending it back to the final response. 

Overview 

  • user triggers the iFlow using an HTTPS sender 
  • The iFlow calls API 1, captures its response
  • With in response of the API 1 it calls API 2, captures its response 
  • Finally, it processes and returns the combined responses of both APIs 

Step-by-Step Implementation 

Step 1: Create an iFlow 

Create a new Integration Flow (iFlow) in SAP Integration Suite as shown in the below figure. 

HTTPS sender --> iFlow logic (it will sequentially call the two APIs) --> HTTP receiver

 

Step 2: Configure the HTTPS Sender Adapter 

  • In the sender side give the address in the connection tab(endpoint path). It is basically the endpoint used to trigger externally (for example in our case it's postman).
  • In our iFlow I have given the address as /testweatherNote: Make sure the address starts with /<address>

 

Step 3: Configure Content Modifier 1 (API 1 Headers and Properties) 

Message Header 

Use content modifier header to externally call the API using it's host and key values.

Add the host and key value of the first API.

 Exchange Properties 

Use exchange properties to fetch the input values dynamically.

  • Name: Input tag name 
  • Source Type: XPath / Expression 
  • Source Value: XML input tag 

This property will later be used in the API call.

 Step 4: Configure Receiver 1 (HTTP Adapter – API 1) 

Based on the input, response is fetched from the external API via request reply and HTTP receiver adapter.

This request reply fetches the response from the external API and send to the next step of the flow.

  • In the Receiver 1 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 1 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step performs the actual call to the first API. 

 Step 5: Configure Content Modifier 2 (Store API 1 Response) 

Add 2nd Content Modifier to store the response of the 1st API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response1 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body} 

This stores the first API response for later use. 

 Step 6: Configure Content Modifier 3 (API 2 Headers)

Now let us call the 2nd API. As done in the earlier step we'll use the content modifier to call the 2nd API

Add the host and key value of the second API.

These headers may differ from API 1 depending on the provider. 

In Exchange property add the properties required to fetch the response from API 2.

Step 7: Configure Receiver 2 (HTTP Adapter – API 2) 

Add request reply and HTTP receiver adapter to fetch the response from the 2nd API.

  • In the Receiver 2 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 2 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step triggers the second API call.

Step 8: Configure Content Modifier 4 (Store API 2 Response)  

Add 4th Content Modifier to store the response of the 2nd API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response2 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body}

This stores the response of the second API. 

Step 9: Configure Content Modifier 5 (Final Message Body) 

Add content modifier 5 to store the responses of Both the APIs. This will give us the combined output of both APIs

In Content Modifier 5 Message Body Set Type: Expression and in body add

  • ${property.response1} 
  • ${property.response2} 

This step prepares the final response that combines or processes both API responses.

Step 10: Save and Deploy the iFlow 

  • Save the iFlow 
  • Deploy it successfully 

Step 11: Test Using Postman 

  • Copy the generated HTTPS endpoint 
  • Paste it into Postman 
  • Send the request 
  • Verify the combined output from both APIs  

 

Conclusion/Summary

In this blog we have covered how can we use multiple APIs in a single iFlow with help of content modifier. Also learnt about how to call external API using content modifier headers and reading the dynamic input from the postman using the content modifier exchange properties. We also saw how to take API 1 response as input for API 2 and in final result using content modifier message body we combined the both APIs responses. By this approach developers can design scalable and reusable iFlows in the real time scenarios.

entry.title: Building a Real‑Time Currency Conversion iFlow using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

While learning SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), I wanted to work on a practical, real‑time use case rather than a basic file‑based or sample integration. In many real projects, applications need to handle multiple currencies because of global customers, suppliers, and financial transactions.

To understand how CPI handles API‑based synchronous integrations, I decided to build a currency conversion iFlow. The objective was to expose an HTTPS endpoint, call an external currency conversion API, and return the converted amount in a readable format. Along with the happy path, I also wanted to implement proper exception handling, similar to what is expected in real production scenarios.

This blog shares my design approach, iFlow structure, and key learnings from implementing this scenario.

Business Requirement

The requirements I considered while designing this iFlow were:

  • Convert an amount from one currency to another in real time
  • Expose the functionality through a secure HTTPS endpoint
  • Fetch up‑to‑date exchange rates from an external API
  • Return the result in a user‑friendly output
  • Handle errors in a centralized and structured way

This closely mimics real‑world requirements where CPI acts as an integration layer between business applications and external services.

High‑Level Solution Overview

I designed the solution using SAP Cloud Integration following a simple API‑to‑API pattern:

  1. An external system sends a currency conversion request.
  2. CPI receives and processes the request.
  3. CPI calls an external currency conversion API.
  4. CPI formats the response as an HTML output.
  5. In case of any failure, CPI handles errors through an Exception Subprocess and sends notifications.

This approach helped me understand how CPI manages synchronous calls, properties, transformations, and error handling together.

iFlow Design Overview

The iFlow is divided into two main parts:

  • Main Integration Flow – handles the successful processing
  • Global Exception Subprocess – handles errors from any step in the flow

This separation made the design clean and easier to maintain.

I-FLow.

Main Integration Flow – Step‑by‑Step

HTTPS_In

I used an HTTPS sender adapter as the entry point of the iFlow. This allows external systems or tools like Postman to send requests in JSON format.

This step starts the message processing and generates the Message Processing Log (MPL) ID, which is useful for monitoring and troubleshooting.

Why I used it:
Most real‑time integrations in CPI are API‑driven, so HTTPS was the natural choice.

HTTPS sender adapter configuration.

You can choose your own address name. 

JSON2XML_Convert

Since the incoming request is in JSON format, I added a JSON to XML converter. This made it easier to work with the payload in CPI, especially for extracting values using content modifiers or XPath.

Learning:
Converting early helps avoid complications in later steps.

Set_ConvParams (Content Modifier)

In this step, I extracted the source currency, target currency, and amount and stored them as Exchange Properties.

I purposely used properties instead of reading values repeatedly from the payload, as this improves readability and reusability across the flow.

Why I did this:
Using properties keeps later steps independent of payload structure.

Content Modifier (Properties tab)

Call_RateAPI

Here, I used a Request‑Reply pattern to call an external currency conversion API. Since the requirement was to return the converted amount immediately, a synchronous call was necessary.

During testing, I intentionally triggered API errors to verify whether my exception handling was working correctly.

Learning:
Request‑Reply is ideal when immediate responses are required.

 

Build_HTML_Result

After receiving the API response, I used a Groovy script to extract the converted amount and build an HTML‑formatted output.

Instead of returning raw JSON or plain text, I formatted the result as an HTML table containing:

  • Source currency
  • Target currency
  • Source amount
  • Converted amount
  • Timestamp (IST)

Why I did this:
HTML output improves readability for users, testers, and demos.

HTTP_Out

Finally, the formatted HTML response is sent back to the caller. This allows the result to be previewed directly in Postman or a browser.

Use case:
This is very helpful during testing and demonstrations without needing a separate UI.

Exception Subprocess – Centralized Error Handling

While building the flow, I realized that handling only the success scenario was not enough. So I implemented a Global Exception Subprocess to handle failures consistently.

Err_Start

This step is triggered automatically whenever an error occurs in any part of the main flow, such as:

  • API failures
  • Groovy errors
  • Payload or conversion issues

Err_Build_HTML

In this step, I extracted error details such as:

  • MPL ID
  • Error type
  • Error message
  • Timestamp

I formatted these details into an HTML table, similar to the success response. This made the error output structured and easy to understand.

Learning:
Readable error messages save a lot of troubleshooting time.

Mail_Out_Err

I added a Mail adapter to send the formatted error details to a support email ID. This ensures that failures are noticed immediately without checking CPI logs manually.

Real‑world relevance:
This is commonly used in production environments for monitoring.

Err_End

The exception flow ends cleanly after sending the notification, ensuring proper message lifecycle completion.

The above one is for incorrect input payload 

Benefits of This Approach

  • Practical understanding of API‑based integrations in SAP CPI
  • Importance of Exchange Properties for clean design
  • How to implement a reusable Exception Subprocess
  • Benefits of HTML‑formatted responses for both success and errors
  • How CPL logs (MPL ID) help in monitoring and support

Conclusion

This exercise helped me gain hands‑on experience in building a real‑time integration scenario using SAP Cloud Integration. By implementing both success and error handling properly, I was able to design an iFlow that closely resembles a real‑world project.

The same design pattern can be reused for many API‑driven integration scenarios, making it a valuable learning experience.

entry.title: Re: CLM - Contract and Lease Management Migration in Publ...
entry.content:
Providing an update here so that it may assist others facing similar migration issue.

 
As per your suggestion @Chris1973  we rechecked everything, particularly the remaining lease payments. 
 
The migrated CLM “contract value” conditions representing the remaining future lease payments were correct however, the opening NPV (and ROU asset) values were overstated due to a manual correction made in the legacy S4 RE-FX contract that caused this inconsistency.
By removing our NPV statistical condition for this contract and allowing CLM to calculate the NPV from migrated cash flows + rate, we were able to see there was a difference between the valuation cashflows in S/4 legacy report and what CLM was calculating for NPV.  This led us to find a manual correction that was done in the S/4 contract that had over inflated the NPV and ROU asset values, leaving a liability balance remaining at the end of the contract term, evident in the valuation cashflow report.
 
As the data had already been migrated via the cockpit, the corrections have been made to the contract master data manually in Production, prior to now activating.  Issue resolved.
 
Thanks for your help
Sonya

entry.title: Re: Invoice ERROR
entry.content:
The error occurs because certain conditions required for processing the invoice via EDI/SOA are not being met. Specifically, the system can only settle invoice line items received multiple times if:

  • The purchase order line item is set up for goods receipt–based invoice verification.
  • The goods receipt number (delivery note number) is included in the incoming IDoc or SOA message.
  • A differential invoice is used, with a previous invoice already existing (so the system can apply the two‑line item concept).

If any of these conditions are missing, the system cancels posting of the IDoc/SOA message.

Next Steps:

  1. Please check the IDoc or SOA message details to confirm whether the goods receipt number and purchase order line item are correct.
  2. If the supplier sent incorrect data (e.g., wrong PO line item), the message may be editable.
  3. If editing is not possible, kindly contact the supplier and request that they resend the invoice with corrected information.
  4. This will ensure the invoice can be processed successfully.

entry.title: Re: Key Figure Editability Horizon in SAP IBP
entry.content:
Hi @renegola92 ,

the editable and non-editable periods for the same Product A are overlapping. This means that the requirements contradict themselves. So if you want to make the periods 12.2025->infinity editable and in the same context 10.2025->infinity (i.e. FY 26), only the union of both works, i.e. 10.2025->infinity. Imho there is no standard way to tell the system for which period level a specific editability horizon shall be applied in case the underlying key figures share the same base planning level.

BR, Peter

entry.title: Building Custom Joule Skills via RFC: Guide for SAP S/4HANA and ECC Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In today’s digital enterprise landscape, rapid access to critical business data can significantly impact operational efficiency and customer satisfaction. SAP’s AI-powered digital assistant, Joule, offers organizations the ability to interact with their enterprise systems through natural language conversations. By extending Joule with custom skills, businesses can create tailored experiences that bring real-time data directly to their teams—without the need for complex navigation through traditional ERP interfaces.

This article provides a comprehensive technical guide for building custom Joule skills that connect to SAP S/4HANA or ECC systems via RFC (Remote Function Call). We’ll explore a practical use case and walk through the complete configuration process.

Business Scenario

Consider a common challenge faced by sales and operations teams: A customer inquires about the manufacturing progress of their order. Traditionally, the specialist would need to log into the ERP system, navigate through multiple screens, and manually locate the production order details. This process is time-consuming and interrupts the flow of customer communication.

With a custom Joule skill, this same specialist can simply ask Joule in natural language—"What’s the status of production order 12345?"—and receive immediate, formatted information directly within SAP Build Workzone. This streamlined approach enhances productivity and improves response times to customer inquiries.

 

 

Solution Architecture

The technical architecture comprises several integrated SAP components:

  • SAP Build Workzone: The user interface where specialists interact with Joule
  • Joule Studio: The development environment for designing and building custom AI skills
  • SAP Build: Used to create Action Projects that expose backend functionality
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP): Hosts destination configurations and orchestrates connections
  • SAP Cloud Connector: Establishes secure RFC connections between BTP and on-premise or private cloud systems
  • SAP S/4HANA / ECC: The backend ERP system containing production order data

 

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Establish RFC Connectivity

The foundation of this solution is a secure connection between your cloud and on-premise environments.

Build RFC Connection with SAP Cloud Connector:
Configure the SAP Cloud Connector to create a secure tunnel between your BTP environment and your S/4HANA or ECC system. This ensures that backend functions can be accessed securely without exposing your entire system to the internet.

 

Setup Destination in BTP Cockpit:
Create a destination in your BTP subaccount that references the RFC connection established through the Cloud Connector. This destination will be consumed by SAP Build to enable communication with your backend system.

 

Step 2: Create a BAPI/RFC Action Project

With connectivity established, the next step is to expose the specific backend functionality that Joule will use.

Create the Action Project:
Navigate to the SAP Build cockpit and create a new BAPI/RFC Action project. Search for the BAPI that retrieves production order information—for example, BAPIs related to production order management.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Test the Connection:
After creating the action project, test the connection to verify that data can be successfully retrieved from your ERP system. By default, these actions operate in READ ONLY mode to protect your business data from unintended modifications.

 

 

Optional Verification with SE37:
For deeper technical validation, you can use transaction code SE37 in your S/4HANA or ECC system to directly test the BAPI function with various parameters. This helps you understand the data structures and responses before integrating them into your Joule skill.

 

 

Step 3: Design Skills in Joule Studio

This is where you design the conversational experience and connect it to your backend data.

Skill 1: Get Production Orders

Each skill consists of three major components:

  1. Skill Description: Define the purpose of the skill and the natural language triggers that will activate it when users interact with Joule.
  2. BAPI Action: Link the skill to the BAPI/RFC Action project you created in SAP Build, establishing the connection between the conversational interface and your backend data.
  3. Send Message: Design the end-user output using the Message Configuration editor. This allows you to transform raw BAPI data into formatted, interactive cards that present information clearly and professionally.

 

 

 

Skill 2: Get Production Order Detail

Following the same pattern, create a second skill that provides detailed information about a specific production order. This allows users to drill down from a list view to detailed information through natural conversation.

 

 

 

Step 4: Deploy and Enable in Workzone

The final phase makes your custom skills accessible to end users.

Create a Shared Environment:
In Joule Studio, create a Shared environment that will serve as the runtime for your deployed skills.

 

 

Deploy Your Joule Project:
Deploy your Joule project to the Shared environment. Once deployed, verify that the project appears under Custom Capabilities.

 

 

Configure Joule in SAP Build Workzone:
Set up the integration between Joule and your SAP Build Workzone site. After completing the integration configuration, enable Joule within your specific Workzone site.

 

 

Test Your Custom Capabilities:
Access your Workzone site, open the Joule client, and test your new custom AI capabilities by asking questions in natural language about production orders.

 

 

Benefits and Outcomes

By implementing custom Joule skills, organizations can achieve:

  • Increased Productivity: Eliminate time-consuming navigation through complex ERP interfaces
  • Improved Customer Service: Enable faster, more accurate responses to customer inquiries
  • Enhanced User Experience: Provide intuitive, conversational access to critical business data
  • Secure Data Access: Maintain enterprise-grade security while democratizing data access
  • Reduced Training Requirements: Lower the learning curve for accessing ERP information

Conclusion

Building custom Joule skills via RFC represents a powerful approach to modernizing how users interact with SAP systems. By combining the conversational AI capabilities of Joule with the deep business logic in S/4HANA or ECC, organizations can create intelligent, context-aware experiences that transform daily operations.

The technical implementation, while requiring careful configuration across multiple components, provides a robust and secure foundation for extending SAP’s AI capabilities to meet specific business needs. As organizations continue their digital transformation journeys, solutions like these demonstrate how AI can be practically applied to solve real business challenges.

Additional Resources

  • Build Your First Joule Skill in Joule Studio
  • Joule Studio CodeJam
  • Build custom Joule skills for SAP and non-SAP systems using Joule Studio

entry.title: Re: How an I traverse thru tables and fill a result table?
entry.content:
Hi,

Copilot is your friend here. I got it to come up with the following example code that hopefully does what you need it to do:

CREATE PROCEDURE MY_SCHEMA::BuildDynamicResult
(
    IN  start_value  INT,
    IN  end_value    INT,
    OUT result       MyResultType
)
LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
AS
BEGIN

    DECLARE lv_sql   NVARCHAR(5000);
    DECLARE lv_id    INT;

    -- Initialize the output table
    result = SELECT * FROM :result LIMIT 0;

    lv_id = :start_value;

    WHILE :lv_id <= :end_value DO
        
        -- Build a dynamic SQL statement
        lv_sql = '
            SELECT ' || :lv_id || ' AS ID,
                   ''Value for ' || :lv_id || ''' AS VALUE
            FROM DUMMY
        ';

        -- Temporary container for dynamic result
        DECLARE lt_dyn TABLE LIKE result;

        lt_dyn = APPLY_FILTER(:result, '1 = 0');  -- or: lt_dyn = SELECT * FROM :result LIMIT 0;

        -- Execute dynamic SQL into lt_dyn
        EXECUTE IMMEDIATE :lv_sql INTO lt_dyn;

        -- Append to output table
        result = 
            SELECT * FROM :result
            UNION ALL
            SELECT * FROM :lt_dyn;

        -- Next iteration
        lv_id = :lv_id + 1;

    END WHILE;

END;

Regards,

Johan

entry.title: Re: How to allocate fixed % (e.g. 4%) via SKF in cumulati...
entry.content:
The recommended Best Practice is

  1. If monthly flexibility is critical, use SKF with sender included as receiver, but implement strong governance. Automate SKF uploads via template. Validate that sender is always included in receiver list. Document the logic clearly for auditors and controllers.
  2. If simplicity and transparency are more important, use fixed Sender Share % and accept monthly cycle maintenance.
  3. For cumulative cycles, avoid mid-year changes in SKF ratios; instead, adjust only prospectively.

entry.title: Re: cost element move to other existing ccs OKTZ does not...
entry.content: Changing GL assignment in OKTZ alone does not move costs from CC A to CC B. You must re‑run actual costing (CKMLCP) so the system rebuilds the cost component split with the new mapping. This is the standard way to “trigger/reset” the GL account move in actual costing.

entry.title: Re: Posting on Header Level WBS (SAP PS Public Cloud)
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the behavior you are seeing is standard. Budgeting in Project System (PS) is always controlled at the overall project (header WBS) level. Even if you want to supplement, transfer, or return budget at a lower‑level WBS, the system requires that the header WBS has a budget assigned first. This is because the header WBS acts as the controlling element for the project’s budget profile. Without a budget at the header, the system cannot validate or distribute budget downwards.

Why posting happened without Header Budget is because, When you post costs (actuals) to a WBS, the system allows it because cost postings are independent of budget availability. However, when you perform budget transactions (supplement, transfer, return), the system checks the budget profile rules. If the header WBS has no budget, the system raises the error: “Please assign or supplement the budget to the Header WBS.”

Hence, to resolve your error, you must first assign or supplement budget (it could be a minimal budget amount) at the header WBS level. Only then can you perform budget transfers or returns at lower WBS elements.

entry.title: Re: Delay one operation of task in maintenance plan
entry.content:
In standard SAP PM/maintenance planning, the scheduling logic is tied to the maintenance plan, not to individual operations inside the task list. That is why when you launch multiple plans referencing the same task list, all operations including the one with the 6‑week cycle, are scheduled together.

Having said that, you cannot delay a single operation independently inside a maintenance plan, so the cleanest solution is to use a strategy plan with multiple packages, which allows weekly operations and the 6‑week operation to be scheduled correctly without manual offsets.

entry.title: Re: Procurement of Services in SAP Ariba
entry.content:
Hi @berrylin,

I see, you want to directly integrate SES from SAP Business Network. 

Below are the answers to the questions you have raised - 

  • Is the ExportServiceSheet web service intended for data from Ariba Buying and Invoicing solutions into SAP ERP systems? - Yes, the webservice is intended to send the SES from SAP Ariba Buying & Invoicing to ERP/S4HANA.
  • Is the ExportServiceSheet web service limited to Ariba Buying and Invoicing only? Yes, it is limited to SAP Ariba Buying & Invoicing which is why when supplier submits SES from SAP Business Network the webservice will not trigger.

entry.title: Introducing the Success Guide for SAP Business Suite: Enabling Transformation and Project Success
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of the Success Guide – Project Experience Sharing for SAP Business Suite, aligned with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

This release marks a significant milestone in the evolution of the Success Guide. What began as focused guidance for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition has now expanded into a Business Suite – oriented framework, offering broader, deeper, and more integrated insights to support complex enterprise transformation journeys.

A Strategic Shift Toward Business Suite Integration

As customers increasingly adopt a suite-first strategy, implementation success depends on more than mastering a single product. It requires a holistic understanding of how solutions interact across the enterprise landscape.

The Success Guide reflects this shift by extending beyond SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to include cross-solution integration guidance across key SAP Business Suite components, such as:

  • SAP Cloud ERP
  • SAP Concur
  • SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network
  • ....

By consolidating insights across these solutions, the guide enables project teams to move from product-level expertise to end-to-end solution thinking, ensuring more cohesive implementations and reduced integration risks.

Built on Real Project Experience and Cross-Team Expertise

The Success Guide is not just theoretical documentation - it is grounded in real-world project experience and built through extensive collaboration across multiple SAP teams. By combining field learnings, delivery experience, and product insights, the guide captures recurring risk patterns, proven mitigation strategies, and actionable recommendations that can be directly applied in implementation projects.

This collective intelligence transforms the guide into a practical decision-support tool for project teams, rather than a static reference document.

Designed to Mitigate Risk and Accelerate Delivery

A core objective of the Success Guide is to proactively address common implementation challenges.

It provides:

  • Identified project risk points and root causes
  • Recommended mitigation actions and best practices
  • Contextualized insights aligned to SAP Activate phases
  • Integration-specific considerations across Business Suite solutions

By leveraging these insights early - especially during the Prepare and Explore phases - teams can significantly reduce rework, avoid late-stage surprises, and maintain project momentum.

Continuous Innovation Through Structured Updates

To remain relevant in a fast-evolving cloud landscape, the Success Guide follows a biannual update cycle, aligned with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition major releases.

Each update incorporates:

  • Lessons learned from recent implementation projects
  • Enhancements to existing guidance based on product evolution
  • Expanded coverage across new scenarios and integrations

Additionally, the document benefits from AI-assisted content validation, improving consistency, accuracy, and scalability of the knowledge base.

This ensures the guide remains current, actionable, and aligned with real customer needs.

More Than Documentation: A Trusted Companion for Project Success

The Success Guide is designed to be more than a knowledge repository. It acts as a trusted companion throughout the project lifecycle, helping teams to:

  • Reduce implementation risk
  • Accelerate time-to-value
  • Improve cross-team alignment
  • Leverage proven experience at scale

Whether you are a consultant, architect, or project manager, the guide provides experience-driven insights to support confident decision-making at every stage.

Access the Success Guide

The latest version of the Success Guide is available via the SAP Digital Library – Success Guide, providing centralized and curated access to all updated content.

entry.title: Re: Transport import failing with activation errors in SAP
entry.content: Between the items "Analyzed" and "Retried", you forgot "Fixed the issues". Nobody can help you if you don't provide both the log and the detailed analysis.

entry.title: Breaking Meows: SAP Translation Hub Unveils Groundbreaking Feline Language Pack
entry.content:
Billions of cats are a key demographic in the homes of decision-makers worldwide. Their feedback on products (from couches to cat food) has, until now, been "lost in translation”. This new service bridges that critical communication gap for the global economy.

Main Highlights:

- Language variants: The model had to be trained on thousands of hours of audio to distinguish between the guttural chirps of a Maine Coon and the high-pitched, demanding yowls of a Siamese. We now have over 42 dialect-specific language packs in beta testing.

- The Ambiguity of the "Meow": Our AI team spent many months differentiating the "I’m hungry" meow from the "I’m bored" meow. They are nearly identical in frequency, but the breakthrough came when we correlated the audio with "tail-position metadata."

- Non-Verbal Integration: Vocalization is only 30% of feline communication. The biggest challenge was interpreting non-verbal cues. Our "SAP Vision AI for Felines" add-on can now interpret these signal and turn them into actionable insights.

😺Disclaimer: No cats were harmed during the testing of these features. All cats who supported were rewarded with pawsome treats. 😺

Learn More

  • SAP Translation Hub Documentation
  • LLM-based Translation Supported Languages
  • Contact us: translationhub@sap.com

Have you been waiting for support for a specific language? Let us know in the comments which languages would help your localization projects!
